counterpoints.claim: 'A single parse-merge-with-defaults pass' is simultaneously simple AND non-breaking as the CLI evolves.
counterpoints.scenario: Ship v1 where merge treats a list key (exclude = ["*.tmp"]) by REPLACING the default list. A user hand-edits to add exclude = [".cache"], expecting it to ADD to shipped defaults because the file visibly contains only their one line. Their edit silently drops every built-in exclusion; nothing errors. The only clean fix is switching that key to list-MERGE, but per 'no breaking changes after release' every existing user who relied on replace-to-clear now has config silently change meaning on upgrade.

result.currentPosition: Single-file with namespaced sections is the correct default; the load-bearing risk is merge-with-defaults semantics, not file count — freeze the merge rule at v1 or the 'non-breaking' promise fails first there.
result.position: The single-file layout in [r1-msg-001] is the right default for a 6-to-12 command CLI, but its strongest assumption — that a single parse-merge-with-defaults pass keeps the loader simple while staying non-breaking — is exactly where it breaks in the field.

proposals.text: Freeze the merge semantics now, before the loader is written: precedence defaults < file, and pin scalar/object override vs list-merge per key. The file format is not what breaks, the merge rule is.
Lock unknown-section preservation to a specific round-trip guarantee: unknown sections AND unknown keys within known sections survive; state explicitly whether comments and ordering survive.

Reserve an optional include / drop-in directory convention in the format spec now (documented, not implemented) so one command can later need machine-generated or secret config without breaking the single-file default.

questions.text: For each key with a non-trivial default, what is the exact v1 merge rule (replace or merge), and is it documented in the file itself?
Does 'preserve unknown sections' extend to unknown keys inside a known section, and to user comments and ordering on write-back?
When a known command's key is malformed, does the single pass fail the whole CLI, fall back silently, or fail only that subcommand?

result.reasoning: Steel-man of [r1-msg-001]: one hand-editable file is genuinely superior at this scale (one artifact to edit/grep/diff/version-control, one discovery path, one precedence story); namespaced [commands.<name>] sections give per-command isolation without per-file sprawl; preserving unknown/future sections satisfies 'no breaking changes after release'; one parse pass is the least machinery that works. The buried assumption is that 'parse-merge-with-defaults' is one simple, atomic, non-breaking operation. The instant defaults are introduced, the file stops being the full truth: effective config is file-plus-defaults computed in code, so 'hand-editable' silently means 'edit half the inputs.' That is the seam where simple and non-breaking quietly stop being compatible.

references.comment: Endorse the single-file namespaced layout, then target the 'parse-merge-with-defaults, simple, non-breaking' assumption.
